clinicalStem: A 52-year-old woman presents with brief episodes of severe spinning dizziness that last 20–30 seconds. The episodes are triggered every time she rolls over in bed or tilts her head back to look upward. She has no tinnitus, hearing loss, or neurological symptoms. On examination, cranial nerves are intact and gait is normal. The Dix-Hallpike maneuver to the right reproduces her symptoms along with upbeat-torsional nystagmus that fatigues after 30 seconds. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Central positional vertigo from posterior fossa lesion
explanation: BPPV is the most common cause of episodic positional vertigo and is characterised by brief (less than 1 minute), position-triggered episodes of vertigo with upbeat-torsional nystagmus on Dix-Hallpike that fatigues — all classic features present here. The absence of hearing loss, tinnitus, and neurological signs excludes Ménière's disease and central causes. Vestibular neuritis causes prolonged, spontaneous vertigo rather than brief positional episodes.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: positional trigger, brief duration, fatiguing upbeat-torsional nystagmus on Dix-Hallpike are pathognomonic of BPPV.
Vestibular neuritis causes acute prolonged spontaneous vertigo (days) without positional trigger and without fatiguing nystagmus.
Ménière's disease causes recurrent attacks lasting 20 minutes to several hours accompanied by fluctuating hearing loss and tinnitus, none of which are present here.
Central positional vertigo (e.g., from a cerebellar tumour) produces non-fatiguing, purely vertical or direction-changing nystagmus and is usually associated with other neurological signs.
Labyrinthitis presents with acute sustained vertigo combined with sensorineural hearing loss, not brief positional episodes without hearing change.
clinicalPearl: BPPV: brief (<1 min) position-triggered vertigo + fatiguing upbeat-torsional nystagmus on Dix-Hallpike — treat with Epley manoeuvre.
keyClue: Brief positional vertigo triggered by head movement with fatiguing upbeat-torsional nystagmus on Dix-Hallpike
commonExamTrap: Confusing BPPV with Ménière's disease — Ménière's attacks last 20 min–several hours and always include fluctuating hearing loss and tinnitus.
missedConcept: The fatiguing, upbeat-torsional nystagmus on Dix-Hallpike is the hallmark distinguishing peripheral (BPPV) from central positional vertigo.

clinicalStem: A 44-year-old accountant presents with four episodes over the past six months of sudden-onset rotatory vertigo, each lasting approximately two hours, associated with a sensation of fullness and low-pitched roaring tinnitus in his left ear. After each episode his hearing partially recovers. Pure-tone audiometry between attacks shows a low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss on the left. He has no focal neurological signs and MRI brain is reported as normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Ménière's disease is diagnosed clinically by the tetrad of episodic vertigo (20 min–12 h), fluctuating low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness — all present here. The recurrent attacks lasting hours, low-pitched tinnitus, aural fullness, and audiometric low-frequency SNHL are characteristic. A normal MRI excludes vestibular schwannoma.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: classic tetrad — episodic vertigo hours-long, fluctuating low-frequency SNHL, tinnitus, and aural fullness.
BPPV causes very brief (seconds) position-triggered vertigo without hearing loss, tinnitus, or aural fullness.
Vestibular schwannoma typically causes unilateral progressive SNHL with imbalance rather than episodic fullblown vertigo attacks; MRI would show a mass in the internal auditory meatus.
Vestibular migraine can mimic Ménière's but lacks the audiometric low-frequency SNHL and aural fullness; headache or photophobia usually accompany the vertigo.
Labyrinthitis presents with a single acute prolonged attack of vertigo plus sudden hearing loss, not recurrent episodic attacks.
clinicalPearl: Ménière's disease = recurrent vertigo episodes 20 min–12 h + ipsilateral low-frequency SNHL + tinnitus + aural fullness; audiometry is essential.
keyClue: Recurrent hours-long vertigo + low-pitched roaring tinnitus + aural fullness + fluctuating low-frequency SNHL on the same side
commonExamTrap: Selecting vestibular schwannoma because it is unilateral — schwannoma causes progressive not episodic deficits and would show a mass on MRI.
missedConcept: Low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss on audiometry is characteristic of endolymphatic hydrops (Ménière's) and distinguishes it from other vestibular disorders.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with severe rotatory vertigo that started abruptly 18 hours ago and has not resolved. She is unable to walk without falling toward the left. She had an upper respiratory tract infection two weeks ago. She has no hearing loss, no tinnitus, and no diplopia, dysphagia, or limb weakness. Examination reveals a spontaneous left-beating horizontal nystagmus that is suppressed by visual fixation. Head impulse test is positive to the left. Which single investigation is most important to perform next to confirm the diagnosis and exclude a central cause?
options: MRI brain with gadolinium (diffusion-weighted and FLAIR sequences)
Serum VZV and HSV-1 IgM titres
explanation: Although the clinical picture (post-viral, spontaneous sustained vertigo, positive HIT to the affected side, nystagmus suppressed by fixation, no hearing loss) strongly suggests vestibular neuritis, the most important investigation is MRI brain with DWI to exclude posterior circulation infarction (lateral medullary or cerebellar stroke), which can closely mimic vestibular neuritis. CT misses posterior fossa ischaemia. Caloric testing confirms reduced canal function but does not exclude stroke.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: MRI with DWI is the gold-standard investigation to exclude posterior fossa stroke, which is the dangerous mimic of vestibular neuritis that must not be missed.
Pure-tone audiogram is important if hearing loss is suspected, but this patient has no hearing symptoms; it does not differentiate vestibular neuritis from a central lesion.

Caloric testing (bithermal) confirms canal paresis on the affected side and supports the diagnosis of vestibular neuritis, but it does not rule out a posterior fossa stroke and is not the most urgent next step.
CT head without contrast has very poor sensitivity for posterior fossa ischaemia due to bone artefact and should not be relied upon to exclude central pathology in this setting.
Viral serology may support a viral aetiology retrospectively but results are slow, do not alter immediate management, and cannot exclude a stroke.
clinicalPearl: Any acute prolonged vertigo — even with a 'typical' peripheral picture — warrants MRI-DWI to exclude posterior fossa stroke before attributing it to vestibular neuritis.
keyClue: Acute sustained vertigo not resolving over 18 hours with a positive head impulse test — must exclude posterior circulation stroke
commonExamTrap: Ordering only caloric testing or audiogram — these support but do not exclude the dangerous central mimic (cerebellar/brainstem infarct).
missedConcept: The HINTS exam (Head-Impulse, Nystagmus type, Test of Skew) guides bedside differentiation, but MRI-DWI remains the definitive investigation to exclude central cause.
redFlagReason: Posterior circulation stroke can present identically to vestibular neuritis; missing it is life-threatening — MRI-DWI is mandatory to exclude this dangerous mimic.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old man wakes up and notices he cannot hear out of his right ear. He describes a sense of fullness and mild tinnitus on the right but denies vertigo, otalgia, or otorrhoea. Otoscopy reveals a normal tympanic membrane bilaterally. A 512 Hz tuning fork placed on the forehead lateralises to the left ear. Weber lateralises left; Rinne is positive (AC > BC) bilaterally. Which investigation should be arranged urgently (within 24–72 hours) to confirm the diagnosis and guide management?
options: MRI internal auditory meati with gadolinium
explanation: Sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL) is defined as ≥30 dB SNHL across ≥3 consecutive frequencies within 72 hours — this patient's clinical findings (acute, unilateral, Weber lateralises to good ear, Rinne positive bilaterally) all point to right-sided SNHL. An urgent pure-tone audiogram is mandatory within 24–72 hours to confirm the degree and configuration of loss, classify it as SSNHL, and guide treatment urgency (immediate high-dose oral/intratympanic steroids). MRI IAM is also important but is secondary and investigates underlying cause rather than confirming the diagnosis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: urgent PTA confirms the diagnosis of SSNHL (≥30 dB across ≥3 frequencies) and quantifies severity, determining whether emergency steroid therapy is warranted immediately.

MRI of the internal auditory meati with gadolinium is essential to exclude a retrocochlear lesion (e.g., vestibular schwannoma) but is ordered after the audiogram confirms SNHL; it does not confirm the diagnosis in the acute phase.
Tympanometry assesses middle ear function and would be normal in SNHL; it cannot confirm a sensorineural loss.
CT temporal bones provides excellent bony detail but poorly assesses the cochlear nerve and cochlea, and exposes the patient to radiation; it does not confirm SNHL.
ABR (BAEP) is useful to characterise a retrocochlear lesion but is not the first-line urgent investigation and cannot replace PTA for confirming and quantifying SSNHL.
clinicalPearl: Sudden SNHL is an ENT emergency — arrange urgent PTA, start oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg within 24 h, and arrange MRI IAM to exclude schwannoma.
keyClue: Acute unilateral hearing loss, Weber lateralises to the contralateral (good) ear, normal otoscopy — points to sensorineural loss requiring audiogram
commonExamTrap: Jumping straight to MRI — the audiogram must come first to confirm and quantify SNHL, because MRI does not diagnose or grade the hearing loss.
missedConcept: SSNHL is a medical emergency; the diagnostic audiogram also dictates urgency — the worse the loss, the more urgent the steroid therapy.

clinicalStem: A 70-year-old retired carpenter presents with gradual, bilateral hearing loss over 10 years, worse in noisy environments. He has tinnitus bilaterally. He worked without hearing protection for 40 years. Otoscopy is entirely normal. Weber tuning fork test (512 Hz) does not lateralise. Rinne test is positive (AC > BC) bilaterally. Audiometry shows a bilateral symmetrical high-frequency dip at 4 kHz ('noise notch'). What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: The bilateral 4 kHz notch on audiometry is the hallmark of noise-induced sensorineural hearing loss (NIHL). The 40-year occupational noise history, bilateral high-frequency loss with the characteristic 4 kHz 'noise notch', normal otoscopy, and bilaterally positive Rinne test (ruling out conductive loss) all confirm NIHL. Presbycusis is an important differential but lacks the specific 4 kHz notch and instead shows a gradual high-frequency slope.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: 40-year noise exposure + bilateral 4 kHz audiometric notch is the pathognomonic pattern for noise-induced SNHL.
Presbycusis (age-related SNHL) causes a gradually sloping high-frequency loss without the characteristic 4 kHz notch; it is a diagnosis of exclusion and lacks an occupational noise history.
Otosclerosis causes a conductive hearing loss (Rinne negative, Weber lateralises to worse ear) with a Carhart notch at 2 kHz on audiometry — not positive Rinne and not a 4 kHz dip.
Chronic suppurative otitis media would present with otorrhoea, visible tympanic membrane perforation, and a conductive loss — not a normal otoscopy.
Bilateral Ménière's is rare and would require recurrent episodes of vertigo, aural fullness, and fluctuating low-frequency (not high-frequency) SNHL.
clinicalPearl: The 4 kHz audiometric 'noise notch' with occupational noise history is diagnostic of noise-induced SNHL — hearing protection prevents further damage.
keyClue: 40-year occupational noise exposure + bilateral 4 kHz audiometric dip ('noise notch')
commonExamTrap: Attributing the loss to presbycusis based on age alone — the 4 kHz notch and occupational history specifically indicate NIHL.
missedConcept: Presbycusis produces a slope from 2 kHz upwards; NIHL specifically notches at 4 kHz with relative preservation of higher frequencies — a subtle but exam-tested distinction.

clinicalStem: A 48-year-old woman is referred by her GP for a three-year history of slowly progressive unilateral right-sided hearing loss and constant high-pitched tinnitus on the right. She reports no vertigo but has noted some imbalance. Otoscopy is normal. Audiometry shows a right-sided asymmetric high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss. Stapedial reflexes are absent on the right at all frequencies. Speech discrimination is disproportionately poor relative to the pure-tone average. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: MRI internal auditory meati with gadolinium
explanation: The combination of unilateral progressive SNHL, absent ipsilateral stapedial reflexes, and disproportionately poor speech discrimination is highly suspicious for a retrocochlear lesion — most commonly a vestibular schwannoma (acoustic neuroma). MRI IAM with gadolinium is the gold-standard investigation, with sensitivity exceeding 97% for detecting intracanalicular tumours, and it directly visualises the vestibulocochlear nerve and the internal auditory canal.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: gadolinium-enhanced MRI IAM has >97% sensitivity for vestibular schwannoma and is the definitive investigation for suspected retrocochlear pathology.

CT temporal bones provides excellent bony detail but has poor soft-tissue resolution for neural structures; it can show bony erosion of the IAM in large tumours but misses small intracanalicular lesions and cannot replace MRI.
ABR can detect retrocochlear lesions (prolonged wave I–V interpeak latency) with ~90–95% sensitivity but is less sensitive than MRI for small tumours; it is used when MRI is contraindicated.
Caloric testing may show unilateral canal paresis consistent with CN VIII dysfunction but cannot image the tumour and is not diagnostic.
Electrocochleography assesses endolymphatic hydrops (Ménière's disease) and is not appropriate for diagnosing a retrocochlear mass.
clinicalPearl: Unilateral SNHL + absent stapedial reflexes + poor speech discrimination = retrocochlear lesion until proven otherwise — order MRI IAM with gadolinium.
keyClue: Asymmetric unilateral SNHL with absent ipsilateral stapedial reflexes and disproportionately poor speech discrimination — hallmarks of retrocochlear pathology
commonExamTrap: Ordering CT temporal bones — CT cannot reliably identify small intracanalicular schwannomas; MRI with gadolinium is the only acceptable investigation.
missedConcept: Absent stapedial reflexes with SNHL and poor speech discrimination specifically point to a retrocochlear (nerve/brainstem) rather than a cochlear lesion.

clinicalStem: A 72-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes presents with a 3-week history of severe right otalgia radiating to the jaw, purulent otorrhoea, and left-sided facial weakness. He has been treated twice with topical antibiotic drops without improvement. On examination, granulation tissue is visible at the bony-cartilaginous junction of the right external auditory canal. Temperature is 38.2 °C, WBC 14.2 × 10⁹/L, and ESR 88 mm/h. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute otitis media with facial nerve palsy
Squamous cell carcinoma of the external auditory canal
explanation: Malignant (necrotising) otitis externa (MOE) is an invasive Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection of the external auditory canal that classically occurs in elderly diabetic or immunocompromised patients. The hallmarks are severe otalgia disproportionate to findings, granulation tissue at the bony-cartilaginous junction, failure to respond to topical drops, systemic inflammation, and cranial nerve (especially facial nerve VII) palsy — all present here. It requires CT/MRI for extent and systemic antipseudomonal therapy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: classic MOE triad — elderly diabetic, granulation at bony-cartilaginous junction, facial nerve palsy, severe otalgia unresponsive to topical drops.
Acute otitis media with facial nerve palsy occurs in the context of AOM (bulging infected TM seen through otoscope) rather than external canal granulation tissue; it is more common in children.

Ramsay Hunt syndrome (herpes zoster oticus) presents with vesicles on the auricle/external canal, facial palsy, and hearing loss — no vesicles are described here and granulation tissue is not a feature.

SCC of the EAC can present with otalgia and bloody/purulent discharge but is not typically associated with acute febrile illness, and granulation tissue at the bony-cartilaginous junction is the sentinel finding of MOE.

Cholesteatoma with intracranial extension causes conductive loss and may erode the facial nerve canal but typically has keratinous debris and a long history; it does not produce external canal granulation tissue in a diabetic in this acute fashion.
clinicalPearl: Elderly diabetic + severe otalgia + granulation tissue at bony-cartilaginous junction of EAC + cranial nerve palsy = malignant OE until proven otherwise; start systemic antipseudomonal antibiotics urgently.
keyClue: Elderly diabetic + granulation tissue at bony-cartilaginous junction + facial nerve palsy + failure of topical treatment
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing simple otitis externa and repeating topical therapy — the granulation tissue at the bony-cartilaginous junction and cranial nerve palsy are red flags for malignant OE.
missedConcept: Malignant OE is not malignant histologically — it is a necrotising infection (Pseudomonas) that spreads to bone (skull base osteomyelitis) and cranial nerves.
redFlagReason: Malignant otitis externa with facial nerve palsy indicates skull base osteomyelitis requiring urgent systemic antipseudomonal antibiotics and imaging — delayed treatment leads to further cranial nerve palsies and death.

clinicalStem: A 19-year-old competitive swimmer presents in August with a 4-day history of progressive right ear pain and itching, worsened when she pulls on her ear or presses on the tragus. Otoscopy reveals an erythematous, oedematous external auditory canal with scant purulent debris; the tympanic membrane is partially obscured but appears intact. She has no fever and her hearing is normal. What is the most likely causative organism?
explanation: Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the most common causative organism in acute diffuse otitis externa ('swimmer's ear'), responsible for approximately 60–70% of cases. Swimming disrupts the cerumen barrier, raises canal pH, and creates a moist environment ideal for Pseudomonas. Staphylococcus aureus is the second most common, but in the context of a swimmer P. aeruginosa is the predominant pathogen.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Pseudomonas aeruginosa accounts for the majority of swimmer's ear cases due to its affinity for warm, moist, alkaline environments.
S. aureus is the second most common cause of acute otitis externa overall and can cause folliculitis/furunculosis in the EAC, but it is not the leading organism in swimmers.
Aspergillus niger causes otomycosis — a fungal OE presenting with black/grey spores and thick debris — typically seen after prolonged antibiotic use, not acute swimmer's ear.
S. pneumoniae is the leading cause of acute otitis media (middle ear), not external otitis.
Candida albicans is associated with fungal otitis externa in immunocompromised patients or after prolonged antibiotics, presenting with white debris and intense pruritus, not acute swimmer's OE.
clinicalPearl: Swimmer's ear (acute diffuse OE) = Pseudomonas aeruginosa; treat with topical acetic acid or ciprofloxacin-hydrocortisone drops and keep canal dry.
keyClue: Competitive swimmer + acute ear pain/itch + tragus tenderness + moist erythematous EAC
commonExamTrap: Selecting S. aureus — while it can cause OE, Pseudomonas is the dominant organism specifically in swimmer's ear.
missedConcept: Water exposure alters EAC pH and disrupts cerumen, favouring Pseudomonas growth — hence routine advice to keep ears dry during treatment.

clinicalStem: A 35-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of persistent left-sided mucopurulent, foul-smelling otorrhoea despite several courses of antibiotics. He reports gradually worsening left conductive hearing loss. On otoscopy, a white pearly mass with keratin debris is seen behind an attic (pars flaccida) retraction pocket of the left tympanic membrane. Rinne is negative on the left; Weber lateralises to the left. No facial weakness or vertigo. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Cholesteatoma is an abnormal accumulation of desquamating keratinising squamous epithelium in the middle ear. The classic presentation is foul-smelling persistent otorrhoea that fails to respond to antibiotics, conductive hearing loss, and a white pearly mass or keratin-filled attic retraction pocket on otoscopy. The attic location (pars flaccida) is characteristic of acquired cholesteatoma. It is 'unsafe' CSOM due to its erosive and potentially life-threatening complications.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: white pearly keratinous debris in a pars flaccida retraction pocket with persistent foul-smelling otorrhoea and conductive loss is the classic picture of acquired cholesteatoma.

Tubotympanic (safe) CSOM presents with a central TM perforation, mucopurulent discharge, and conductive loss but WITHOUT the white keratinous mass or attic retraction — the discharge has no foul odour specific to keratin breakdown.
Tympanosclerosis (hyalinised calcification of the TM and middle ear) appears as white chalky plaques on the TM but does not cause persistent otorrhoea or a retraction pocket with keratin.
Glomus tympanicum (paraganglioma) presents as a red/blue pulsatile mass visible through the TM with pulsatile tinnitus — not a white keratinous lesion.
Acute otitis media is a short-duration painful infection with a bulging TM; it does not present with a months-long history of foul otorrhoea or a white pearly mass.
clinicalPearl: Foul-smelling otorrhoea resistant to antibiotics + white/pearly mass in attic retraction pocket = cholesteatoma; it is 'unsafe' CSOM that requires surgery.
keyClue: White pearly keratinous mass in pars flaccida (attic) retraction pocket with foul-smelling persistent otorrhoea
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing safe (tubotympanic) CSOM — the attic location and white keratinous debris are specific to cholesteatoma, not the safe type.
missedConcept: Cholesteatoma is 'unsafe' CSOM because it erodes bone via collagenase enzymes, risking facial nerve palsy, labyrinthine fistula, and intracranial complications.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman presents with bilateral, slowly progressive hearing loss that she notices is worse when she is pregnant. Her mother had the same problem and had surgery in her 40s. On examination, otoscopy is normal. Rinne test is negative (BC > AC) bilaterally; Weber does not lateralise. She has no tinnitus or vertigo. Audiometry is requested. Which audiometric finding would most strongly support the suspected diagnosis?
options: Bilateral conductive hearing loss with a Carhart notch at 2 kHz on bone conduction
Bilateral sensorineural hearing loss with a 4 kHz dip
Bilateral conductive hearing loss with a flat Type B tympanogram
Bilateral mixed hearing loss with absent acoustic reflexes and elevated bone conduction thresholds at 4 kHz
explanation: Otosclerosis is an autosomal dominant disease causing abnormal bone remodelling of the otic capsule, fixing the stapes footplate and producing a progressive conductive hearing loss. The characteristic audiometric finding is a conductive loss with the 'Carhart notch' — a mechanical artefact at 2 kHz on bone conduction testing that corrects after stapedectomy. Normal otoscopy, bilateral negative Rinne, family history, and worsening in pregnancy (due to oestrogen effects) are hallmarks.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: bilateral conductive loss + Carhart notch at 2 kHz on bone conduction is pathognomonic of otosclerosis.
Bilateral 4 kHz SNHL is the pattern of noise-induced hearing loss, not otosclerosis; otosclerosis produces conductive not sensorineural loss.
Flat Type B tympanogram indicates middle ear effusion (glue ear) — in otosclerosis the tympanogram is typically Type As (shallow peak, reduced compliance) due to stapes fixation, not flat.
Unilateral low-frequency SNHL is characteristic of Ménière's disease, not otosclerosis.

Bilateral mixed loss with elevated bone conduction thresholds at 4 kHz could represent cochlear otosclerosis (rare, advanced) but the 4 kHz BC elevation is not the primary diagnostic marker; the Carhart notch at 2 kHz is the classic finding.
clinicalPearl: Otosclerosis: young woman + bilateral conductive loss + Carhart notch at 2 kHz on BC + normal otoscopy + family history + worsens in pregnancy — treat with stapedectomy.
keyClue: Young woman + bilateral negative Rinne + normal otoscopy + family history + worsening in pregnancy
commonExamTrap: Selecting Type B tympanogram — otosclerosis gives Type As (reduced compliance/shallow peak), not flat; flat suggests effusion.
missedConcept: The Carhart notch (2 kHz BC dip) is a mechanical artefact of stapes fixation — it reverses after stapedectomy, confirming conductive rather than cochlear origin.

clinicalStem: A 6-year-old boy presents with a 10-day history of left ear pain and fever. His parents completed a course of amoxicillin 5 days ago but he is now worse. On examination, temperature is 39.1 °C, the left pinna is displaced anteriorly and inferiorly, there is tender swelling and erythema over the left mastoid process obliterating the post-auricular sulcus, and the posterior superior wall of the external canal is sagging. Left tympanic membrane is dull and erythematous with reduced mobility. What is the most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis and assess extent?
options: High-resolution CT of the temporal bones with contrast
explanation: This child has acute mastoiditis — a serious complication of AOM. The signs of post-auricular erythema/tenderness with pinna displacement, sagging of the posterior canal wall, and failure of antibiotic therapy confirm the diagnosis clinically, but high-resolution CT temporal bones with contrast is the investigation of choice to confirm cortical bone erosion, assess whether there is a subperiosteal abscess or intracranial extension (sigmoid sinus thrombosis, extradural abscess), and guide surgical planning (mastoidectomy).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: CT temporal bones with contrast confirms bone destruction, identifies subperiosteal or intracranial abscess, and guides surgical planning for acute mastoiditis.
Plain X-ray mastoid (Towne's or Law's view) is outdated and has poor sensitivity for cortical bone erosion and cannot assess for intracranial complications — CT has replaced it.

MRI brain with gadolinium is the best modality for intracranial soft-tissue complications (e.g., meningitis, brain abscess, sigmoid sinus thrombosis) but is less readily available in an emergency and does not assess bony cortical destruction as well as CT; CT is the first-line modality.
Pure-tone audiogram quantifies any associated conductive loss but does not confirm mastoiditis, identify abscesses, or guide surgical management.
Tympanocentesis (myringotomy) with culture guides antibiotic therapy and is an important therapeutic step but does not visualise bone destruction or exclude intracranial complications.
clinicalPearl: Acute mastoiditis: displaced pinna + post-auricular swelling + sagging posterior canal wall after AOM — urgent CT temporal bones to assess bone erosion and intracranial extension before mastoidectomy.
keyClue: Pinna displaced anteroinferiorly + post-auricular swelling obliterating sulcus + sagging of posterior canal wall after failed antibiotic treatment of AOM
commonExamTrap: Ordering plain X-ray mastoid — CT has entirely replaced plain films for diagnosing mastoiditis and assessing complications.
missedConcept: The sagging posterior canal wall is a critical sign of acute mastoiditis indicating periosteal and cortical bone involvement, mandating urgent CT and surgical planning.
redFlagReason: Acute mastoiditis with subperiosteal abscess can extend intracranially (meningitis, sigmoid sinus thrombosis, extradural abscess) — urgent CT and IV antibiotics with surgical review are mandatory.

clinicalStem: A 65-year-old woman presents with a 3-day history of severe right-sided otalgia followed by appearance of painful vesicles on the right auricle and external auditory canal. She now has right-sided facial weakness (House-Brackmann grade IV), reduced lacrimation on the right, and hyperacusis. She also reports decreased hearing on the right and mild vertigo. Otoscopy confirms vesicles on the pinna and in the external canal; the facial palsy involves both upper and lower face. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute otitis media with facial nerve involvement
Malignant otitis externa with facial nerve palsy
Cholesteatoma eroding the facial nerve canal
explanation: Ramsay Hunt syndrome is caused by reactivation of varicella-zoster virus (VZV) in the geniculate ganglion of the facial nerve (CN VII). The classic triad is: (1) painful auricular vesicles (zoster oticus), (2) ipsilateral lower motor neuron facial palsy, and (3) audiovestibular symptoms (hearing loss, vertigo). It causes a more severe and longer-lasting facial palsy than Bell's palsy and requires urgent antiviral (aciclovir) plus corticosteroid therapy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: painful auricular vesicles + LMN facial palsy + ipsilateral SNHL + vertigo = Ramsay Hunt syndrome (VZV reactivation in geniculate ganglion).
Bell's palsy is idiopathic LMN facial palsy and is a diagnosis of exclusion — it does not cause vesicles, hearing loss, or vertigo; the presence of vesicles specifically defines Ramsay Hunt.
Acute otitis media can rarely cause facial nerve palsy through the middle ear dehiscence but does not produce vesicles on the auricle or audiovestibular symptoms of this combination.
Malignant OE with facial palsy occurs in elderly diabetics with granulation tissue in the EAC and no vesicles; the context is entirely different (no diabetes mentioned, acute vesicular eruption).
Cholesteatoma eroding the facial nerve canal causes gradual progressive facial palsy associated with foul otorrhoea and a white pearly mass — not an acute vesicular eruption with vertigo.
clinicalPearl: Ramsay Hunt syndrome triad: auricular vesicles + ipsilateral LMN VII palsy + SNHL/vertigo — treat immediately with aciclovir + prednisolone for best chance of facial nerve recovery.
keyClue: Painful vesicles on the auricle and EAC + ipsilateral LMN facial palsy + hearing loss + vertigo
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing Bell's palsy and withholding antivirals — the vesicles are the critical distinguishing feature mandating antiviral therapy in addition to steroids.
missedConcept: Ramsay Hunt has a worse prognosis than Bell's palsy; VZV causes more extensive nerve damage — only 50% achieve full facial recovery vs ~70–80% in Bell's palsy.
